description:
You are acting as a DevSecOps engineer who must quickly enforce a “policy-as-code” rule:  
“All vulnerabilities whose severity is CRITICAL must be collected in their own file that contains only the CVE identifier, the severity word, and the affected package name—in that specific column order and comma-separated.”

The system already contains one CSV report located at  

    /home/user/reports/vuln_report.csv  

1. Inspect that report and, using any combination of the classic GNU text utilities cut, grep, paste, and/or sort (no programming languages), create a **new** file  

    /home/user/reports/policy/critical_vulns.csv  

   that fulfils **exactly** these formatting rules:  
   • It uses a single header row that reads (in upper-case and separated by commas)  
     CVE_ID,SEVERITY,AFFECTED_PACKAGE  
   • Beneath the header, include every row from the source file whose SEVERITY column equals the literal word CRITICAL (case-sensitive match).  
   • Preserve the original line order of those matching rows.  
   • Show only the three columns in the order: CVE_ID (column 1), SEVERITY (column 2), AFFECTED_PACKAGE (column 4).  
   • The file must end with a single trailing newline.  
   • No spaces are allowed before or after any comma.

2. To document compliance, append a one-line log entry to  

    /home/user/reports/action.log  

   following this exact template ( **one space** separates the timestamp from the dash and from the message text):  

        YYYY-MM-DD_HH:MM:SS - generated critical_vulns.csv  

   • Use the current UTC time of execution (24-hour clock, zero-padded).  
   • Do **not** modify or delete any previous lines in the log file. If the log file does not yet exist, your command sequence must create it with this first entry.

3. Use no more than 3-4 terminal commands in total to accomplish both objectives.

The automated tests will check:  
• the precise path, presence, and byte-for-byte contents of /home/user/reports/policy/critical_vulns.csv;  
• the presence of an additional, correctly formatted line at the very end of /home/user/reports/action.log.

A quick manual spot-check in the terminal (e.g., using cat) is allowed but not required—only the resulting files matter.
truth:
Pre-existing directory structure and files:

1. /home/user/reports/vuln_report.csv  
   (exact 6 lines, UNIX line endings)  
   ```
   CVE_ID,SEVERITY,DESCRIPTION,AFFECTED_PACKAGE
   CVE-2023-1111,MEDIUM,OpenSSL minor issue,openssl
   CVE-2023-2222,CRITICAL,Kernel privilege escalation,linux-kernel
   CVE-2023-3333,LOW,Typo in man page,man-db
   CVE-2023-4444,CRITICAL,Remote code execution,libssh
   CVE-2023-5555,HIGH,Potential DoS in zlib,zlib
   ```

2. /home/user/reports/      (directory exists, writable by user)

3. /home/user/reports/policy/  
   • Directory exists and is writable, but initially empty.

4. /home/user/reports/action.log  
   • May or may not exist when the agent starts.  
   • If it exists, it may already contain one or more lines that follow the exact timestamp format shown in the task description; the agent must keep those intact and append a new line.

Expected artefacts after successful completion:

A. /home/user/reports/policy/critical_vulns.csv  
   Exact contents (4 lines, trailing newline):  
   ```
   CVE_ID,SEVERITY,AFFECTED_PACKAGE
   CVE-2023-2222,CRITICAL,linux-kernel
   CVE-2023-4444,CRITICAL,libssh
   ```

B. /home/user/reports/action.log  
   • The file must end with a new UTC timestamped line that matches the regex  
     ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}_[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} - generated critical_vulns\.csv\n$  
   • Any existing lines above it remain untouched.

Permissions: all files/directories are within /home/user and writable by the regular user. No root privileges needed.  
The automated grader will validate both the CSV file and the final line in the log.
